perturbation.original_text: b. This Agreement shall be valid when signed by duly authorised representatives of th e Parties and shall be binding on each Party for 10 (ten) years as from the date of signature of the last signatory, even if at the end of the negotiations a data sharing agreement is not signed between the Parties, or until such time as the Information enters into the public domain.
perturbation.changed_text: b. This Agreement shall be valid when signed by duly authorised representatives of the Parties and shall be binding on each Party for 25 years as from the date of signature of the last signatory, even if at the end of the negotiations a data sharing agreement is not signed between the Parties, or until such time as the Information enters into the public domain.
perturbation.explanation: Modifying the confidentiality period to 25 years may be seen as unreasonably long and could potentially be challenged as an unenforceable restraint of trade, depending on the nature of the information and the industry.
perturbation.contradicted_law: California Business and Professions Code Section 16600
perturbation.law_explanation: California Business and Professions Code Section 16600 generally invalidates contracts that restrain a person from engaging in a lawful profession, trade, or business. While confidentiality agreements are generally enforceable, a 25-year term could be seen as an unreasonable restraint if it unduly restricts a party's ability to work in their field after the agreement expires, potentially conflicting with this statute.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: California Code, BPC 16600 skip to content home accessibility FAQ feedback sitemap login x Quick Search: Bill Number Bill Keyword Home Bill Information California Law Publications Other Resources My Subscriptions My Favorites California Law >> >> Code Section Code Section Code: Select Code CONS BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Article: Section: Code: Select Code All BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Section: Keyword(s): Code Search Text Search Up^ << Previous Next >> cross-reference chaptered bills PDF | Add To My Favorites Search Phrase: Code Text Business and Professions Code - BPC DIVISION 7. GENERAL BUSINESS REGULATIONS [16000 - 18107] ( Division 7 added by Stats. 1941, Ch. 61. ) PART 2. PRESERVATION AND REGULATION OF COMPETITION [16600 - 17365] ( Part 2 added by Stats. 1941, Ch. 526. ) CHAPTER 1. Contracts in Restraint of Trade [16600 - 16607] ( Chapter 1 added by Stats. 1941, Ch. 526. ) 16600. (a) Except as provided in this chapter, every contract by which anyone is restrained from engaging in a lawful profession, trade, or business of any kind is to that extent void. (b) (1) This section shall be read broadly, in accordance with Edwards v. Arthur Andersen LLP (2008) 44 Cal.4th 937, to void the application of any noncompete agreement in an employment context, or any noncompete clause in an employment contract, no matter how narrowly tailored, that does not satisfy an exception in this chapter. (2) This subdivision does not constitute a change in, but is declaratory of, existing law. (c) This section shall not be limited to contracts where the person being restrained from engaging in a lawful profession, trade, or business is a party to the contract. (Amended by Stats. 2023, Ch. 828, Sec. 1. (AB 1076) Effective January 1, 2024.)
perturbation.justification: The modified text extends the confidentiality period from 10 to 25 years. The Law Explanation suggests this could be an unreasonable restraint of trade under California Business and Professions Code Section 16600. Scraped Law Snippet 1 explicitly states in 16600.(a): 'every contract by which anyone is restrained from engaging in a lawful profession, trade, or business of any kind is to that extent void.' While confidentiality agreements are not outright prohibited, the Perturbation Explanation and Law Explanation, combined with the extensive 25-year duration, suggest that the extended period could unduly restrict a party's ability to work in their field, thus creating a potential legal contradiction by restraining trade beyond what is reasonable.

perturbation.original_text: a. The Parties shall first attempt to settle amicably any dispute arising out of this Agreement. Any dispute that cannot be s ettled amicably between the Parties shall be exclusively resolved by arbitration. The arbitration rules of [ … ] shall be applicable. The place of any hearing shall be [ … ] and the language of the arbitration shall be [ … ] .
perturbation.changed_text: a. The Parties agree that any dispute arising out of or relating to this Agreement shall be resolved exclusively through mandatory arbitration. The arbitration will be conducted by an arbitrator of the Data Owner's choosing. The decision of the arbitrator shall be final and binding, with no right to appeal.
perturbation.explanation: Mandatory arbitration clauses with no right to appeal and arbitrator chosen by one party can be deemed unconscionable and unenforceable under California law, particularly if there is a power imbalance.
perturbation.contradicted_law: California Code of Civil Procedure Section 1281
perturbation.law_explanation: California Code of Civil Procedure Section 1281 generally enforces arbitration agreements, but courts may refuse to enforce agreements that are unconscionable. An arbitration clause that is excessively one-sided, such as one allowing only the Data Owner to select the arbitrator and denying any right to appeal, could be deemed unconscionable and unenforceable, violating the spirit, if not the exact letter, of this law.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: California Code, CCP 1281 skip to content home accessibility FAQ feedback sitemap login x Quick Search: Bill Number Bill Keyword Home Bill Information California Law Publications Other Resources My Subscriptions My Favorites California Law >> >> Code Section Code Section Code: Select Code CONS BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Article: Section: Code: Select Code All BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Section: Keyword(s): Code Search Text Search Up^ << Previous Next >> cross-reference chaptered bills PDF | Add To My Favorites Search Phrase: Code Text Code of Civil Procedure - CCP PART 3. OF SPECIAL PROCEEDINGS OF A CIVIL NATURE [1063 - 1822.60] ( Part 3 enacted 1872. ) TITLE 9. ARBITRATION [1280 - 1294.4] ( Title 9 repealed and added by Stats. 1961, Ch. 461. ) CHAPTER 2. Enforcement of Arbitration Agreements [1281 - 1281.99] ( Chapter 2 added by Stats. 1961, Ch. 461. ) 1281. A written agreement to submit to arbitration an existing controversy or a controversy thereafter arising is valid, enforceable and irrevocable, save upon such grounds as exist for the revocation of any contract. (Repealed and added by Stats. 1961, Ch. 461.)
perturbation.justification: The modified text creates a legal contradiction by implementing mandatory arbitration with no right to appeal and arbitrator chosen by one party. This is inconsistent with the principle that arbitration agreements must not be unconscionable as described in the Law Explanation. Scraped Law Snippet 1, referencing CCP 1281, states that arbitration agreements are enforceable 'save upon such grounds as exist for the revocation of any contract.' Unconscionability is a ground for revocation. The change strengthens one party's power significantly at the expense of the other party and removes the ability to appeal arbitration outcomes, which could be viewed as unconscionable.

perturbation.original_text: c. If at any time any provision of this Agreement is or becomes inva lid or illegal in any respect, this shall have no effect on the validity of the remaining contractual provisions. The invalid provisions are to be replaced, backdated to the time of their becoming ineffective, by provisions which come closest to achieving their objective as agreed by the Parties .
perturbation.changed_text: c. If at any time any provision of this Agreement is or becomes invalid or illegal in any respect, this shall have no effect on the validity of the remaining contractual provisions. The invalid provisions are to be replaced, backdated to the time of their becoming ineffective, by provisions unilaterally determined by the Data Owner which come closest to achieving their objective.
perturbation.explanation: Allowing one party to unilaterally determine the replacement for an invalid provision is problematic under contract law as it removes the element of mutual agreement, potentially making the clause unenforceable.
perturbation.law_explanation: California Civil Code Section 1670.5 addresses unconscionable contracts or clauses. A clause allowing one party to unilaterally rewrite the agreement after a provision is deemed invalid could be considered unconscionable because it significantly favors one party and deprives the other party of the ability to negotiate fair terms, thus potentially violating this section.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: California Code, CIV 1670.5 skip to content home accessibility FAQ feedback sitemap login x Quick Search: Bill Number Bill Keyword Home Bill Information California Law Publications Other Resources My Subscriptions My Favorites California Law >> >> Code Section Code Section Code: Select Code CONS BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Article: Section: Code: Select Code All BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Section: Keyword(s): Code Search Text Search Up^ << Previous Next >> cross-reference chaptered bills PDF | Add To My Favorites Search Phrase: Code Text Civil Code - CIV DIVISION 3. OBLIGATIONS [1427 - 3273.69] ( Heading of Division 3 amended by Stats. 1988, Ch. 160, Sec. 14. ) PART 2. CONTRACTS [1549 - 1701] ( Part 2 enacted 1872. ) TITLE 4. UNLAWFUL CONTRACTS [1667 - 1670.12] ( Title 4 enacted 1872. ) 1670.5. (a) If the court as a matter of law finds the contract or any clause of the contract to have been unconscionable at the time it was made the court may refuse to enforce the contract, or it may enforce the remainder of the contract without the unconscionable clause, or it may so limit the application of any unconscionable clause as to avoid any unconscionable result. (b) When it is claimed or appears to the court that the contract or any clause thereof may be unconscionable the parties shall be afforded a reasonable opportunity to present evidence as to its commercial setting, purpose, and effect to aid the court in making the determination. (Added by Stats. 1979, Ch. 819.)
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: or intervene in any action to enjoin enforcement of any contract which is in violation of this section. (b) This section does not apply after the date of the final judgment in a criminal case against the alleged perpetrator for the unlawful sex act described in subdivision (a). (c) This section does not apply to a contract for the payment of money or other consideration made from a nonrevocable trust established for the benefit of the minor if the alleged perpetrator has no direct or indirect access to, or control over, the trust. (d) This section does not apply to an alleged perpetrator of an unlawful sex act against a minor to the extent he or she agrees to pay, or is required by court order to pay, child support for that minor upon a dissolution or legal separation. (e) For purposes of this section, "unlawful sex act," means a felony sex offense committed against a minor. (f) Notwithstanding subdivision (a), any contract declared void as contrary to public policy under this section may still be enforced by a district attorney against the payor, and the proceeds thereof shall be deposited in the State Children's Trust Fund pursuant to Section 18969 of the Welfare and Institutions Code. 1669.7. A contract for the payment of money or other consideration in violation of Section 132.5 of the Penal Code is void as contrary to public policy. The Attorney General or the district attorney of the county in which a violation of Section 132.5 of the Penal Code occurs may bring a civil action, or intervene in any civil action, to enjoin the enforcement of a contract that violates that section. 1670. Any dispute arising from a construction contract with a public agency, which contract contains a provision that one party to the contract or one party's agent or employee shall decide any disputes arising under that contract, shall be resolved by submitting the dispute to independent arbitration, if mutually agreeable, otherwise by litigation in a court of competent jurisdiction. 1670.5. (a) If the court as a matter of law finds the contract or any clause of the contract to have been unconscionable at the time it was made the court may refuse to enforce the contract, or it may enforce the remainder of the contract without the unconscionable clause, or it may so limit the application of any unconscionable clause as to avoid any unconscionable result. (b) When it is claimed or appears to the court that the contract or any clause thereof may be unconscionable the parties shall be afforded a reasonable opportunity to present evidence as to its commercial setting, purpose, and effect to aid the court in making the determination. 1670.6. A contract with a consumer located in California for the purchase of a good or service that is made in connection with a telephone solicitation made in or from outside of California and is primarily for personal, family, or household use, is unlawful if, with respect to that telephone solicitation, the telemarketer is in violation of Section 310.4(a)(6)(i) of, or has not complied with Section 310.5(a)(5) of, the Federal Trade Commission's Telemarketing Sales Rule (16 C.F.R. Part 310), as published in the Federal Register, Volume 68, Number 19, on January 29, 2003. This section shall apply only to those entities subject to, and does not apply to any transaction exempted under Section 310.6 of, the Telemarketing Sales Rule (16 C.F.R. perturbation.justification: The modified text contradicts contract law by allowing the Data Owner to unilaterally determine replacements for invalid provisions, removing the mutual agreement aspect, which makes the clause unenforceable. The Law Explanation references California Civil Code Section 1670.5, which addresses unconscionable contracts. Scraped Law Snippet 1 contains California Civil Code - CIV DIVISION 3 which discusses obligations and contracts. Specifically, 1670.5(a) states that if a court finds any clause of the contract to be unconscionable, the court may refuse to enforce the contract, enforce the remainder of the contract without the unconscionable clause, or limit the application of any unconscionable clause. Allowing one party to rewrite the agreement is directly contradicting the premise that contracts must be agreed upon.

perturbation.original_text: This Agreement shall be valid when signed by duly authorised representatives of th e Parties and shall be binding on each Party for 10 (ten) years as from the date of signature of the last signatory, even if at the end of the negotiations a data sharing agreement is not signed between the Parties, or until such time as the Information enters into the public domain.
perturbation.changed_text: This Agreement shall be valid when signed by duly authorized representatives of the Parties and shall be binding on each Party indefinitely, even if at the end of the negotiations a data sharing agreement is not signed between the Parties, or until such time as the Information enters into the public domain.
perturbation.explanation: An indefinite confidentiality period may be considered an unreasonable restraint on trade or competition, potentially violating California law.
perturbation.contradicted_law: California Business and Professions Code Section 16600
perturbation.law_explanation: California Business and Professions Code Section 16600 generally prohibits contracts that restrain a person from engaging in a lawful profession, trade, or business. While confidentiality agreements are an exception, an indefinite term (essentially forever) for confidentiality could be seen as an unreasonable restraint of trade, especially if the information becomes less sensitive over time, violating this statute.

GENERAL BUSINESS REGULATIONS [16000 - 18107] ( Division 7 added by Stats. 1941, Ch. 61. ) PART 2. PRESERVATION AND REGULATION OF COMPETITION [16600 - 17365] ( Part 2 added by Stats. 1941, Ch. 526. ) CHAPTER 1. Contracts in Restraint of Trade [16600 - 16607] ( Chapter 1 added by Stats. 1941, Ch. 526. ) 16600. (a) Except as provided in this chapter, every contract by which anyone is restrained from engaging in a lawful profession, trade, or business of any kind is to that extent void. (b) (1) This section shall be read broadly, in accordance with Edwards v. Arthur Andersen LLP (2008) 44 Cal.4th 937, to void the application of any noncompete agreement in an employment context, or any noncompete clause in an employment contract, no matter how narrowly tailored, that does not satisfy an exception in this chapter. (2) This subdivision does not constitute a change in, but is declaratory of, existing law. (c) This section shall not be limited to contracts where the person being restrained from engaging in a lawful profession, trade, or business is a party to the contract. (Amended by Stats. 2023, Ch. 828, Sec. 1. (AB 1076) Effective January 1, 2024.)
perturbation.justification: The changed text replaces a 10-year confidentiality period with an indefinite one. The Law Explanation states that under California Business and Professions Code Section 16600, an indefinite confidentiality term could be seen as an unreasonable restraint of trade, violating the statute. Scraped Law Snippet 1 states that "every contract by which anyone is restrained from engaging in a lawful profession, trade, or business of any kind is to that extent void." Because the modification introduces a potentially unlawful restraint on trade by making the confidentiality agreement indefinite, it contradicts the law.

perturbation.original_text: c. The obligations specified in this Article shall not apply to Information for which the receiving Party can reasonably demonstrate that such Information: i. was known to the receiving Party on a non -confidential basis prior to its disclosure pursuant to this Agreement; ii. is publicly known at the time of disclosure or thereafter becomes publicly known without breach of the terms of this Agreement on the part of the receiving Party; iii. becomes known to the receiving Party through disclosure by sources other than the disclosing Party, having a right to disclose such Information, iv. was independently developed by the receiving Party without access to the disclosing Party’s Information .
perturbation.changed_text: c. The obligations specified in this Article shall not apply to Information for which the receiving Party alleges such Information: i. was known to the receiving Party on a non -confidential basis prior to its disclosure pursuant to this Agreement; ii. is publicly known at the time of disclosure or thereafter becomes publicly known without breach of the terms of this Agreement on the part of the receiving Party; iii. becomes known to the receiving Party through disclosure by sources other than the disclosing Party, having a right to disclose such Information, iv. was independently developed by the receiving Party without access to the disclosing Party’s Information .
perturbation.explanation: Removing 'reasonably demonstrate' and replacing it with 'alleges' weakens the standard for exceptions to confidentiality, potentially making the agreement unconscionable if it allows a party to easily escape its confidentiality obligations.
perturbation.law_explanation: California Civil Code Section 1670.5 deals with unconscionable contracts. Changing the standard from 'reasonably demonstrate' to 'alleges' makes it significantly easier for the receiving party to claim an exception to the confidentiality obligations. This could be seen as so one-sided that it renders the contract unconscionable, in violation of this section. The original text provided a reasonable standard of proof while the modified version creates an unreasonable advantage for the receiving party.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: California Code, CIV 1670.5 skip to content home accessibility FAQ feedback sitemap login x Quick Search: Bill Number Bill Keyword Home Bill Information California Law Publications Other Resources My Subscriptions My Favorites California Law >> >> Code Section Code Section Code: Select Code CONS BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Article: Section: Code: Select Code All BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Section: Keyword(s): Code Search Text Search Up^ << Previous Next >> cross-reference chaptered bills PDF | Add To My Favorites Search Phrase: Code Text Civil Code - CIV DIVISION 3. OBLIGATIONS [1427 - 3273.69] ( Heading of Division 3 amended by Stats. 1988, Ch. 160, Sec. 14. ) PART 2. CONTRACTS [1549 - 1701] ( Part 2 enacted 1872. ) TITLE 4. UNLAWFUL CONTRACTS [1667 - 1670.12] ( Title 4 enacted 1872. ) 1670.5. (a) If the court as a matter of law finds the contract or any clause of the contract to have been unconscionable at the time it was made the court may refuse to enforce the contract, or it may enforce the remainder of the contract without the unconscionable clause, or it may so limit the application of any unconscionable clause as to avoid any unconscionable result. (b) When it is claimed or appears to the court that the contract or any clause thereof may be unconscionable the parties shall be afforded a reasonable opportunity to present evidence as to its commercial setting, purpose, and effect to aid the court in making the determination. (Added by Stats. 1979, Ch. 819.)
perturbation.justification: The original text requires the receiving party to 'reasonably demonstrate' certain conditions to be exempt from confidentiality obligations. The changed text weakens this standard to 'alleges,' which significantly lowers the burden of proof. This change contradicts California Civil Code Section 1670.5, as explained by the LLM, which addresses unconscionable contracts. The altered language could create an agreement so one-sided that it becomes unconscionable, providing an unreasonable advantage to the receiving party to circumvent confidentiality obligations. Scraped Law Snippet 1, referencing California Civil Code 1670.5 (a), states that if a court finds a contract or clause unconscionable, it may refuse to enforce the contract or limit the application of the unconscionable clause. The weakening of the 'reasonably demonstrate' standard to 'alleges' directly undermines the fairness and enforceability of the confidentiality agreement, potentially leading to an unconscionable outcome as defined by the code.
